coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots as a turn-on fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of Cd2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:description: In this study, fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized for the first time using ammonium citrate and glutamic acid as precursors via a one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized carbon dots emit blue fluorescence at 436 nm (excited at 320 nm) and demonstrate excellent photobleaching resistance and fluorescence stability in high salt environments. Within the range of 1–25 μM, the fluorescence of CDs gradually increases with the increasing concentration of Cd2+, reaching a limit of detection as low as 13 nM. This phenomenon could be ascribed to the chelation-enhanced fluorescence, a result of Cd2+ forming complexes with the abundant surface functional groups such as CN–, –COOH, –OH, –NH2 in CDs. Furthermore, this turn-on fluorescent probe has been successfully used for the detection of Cd2+ in tap water and lake water, providing an efficient and sensitive method for the analysis of environmental metals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Turn-on fluorescent probe
Cd2+ detection
Chelation enhanced fluorescence

Selectivity and sensitivity of detection of Cd2+
Detection of Cd2+ in real samples
Detection of Cd2+ using by the turn-on fluorescent probe
Possible detection mechanism for CDs towards Cd2+
Detections of Cd2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of CDs at 20 nm; (b) Size distribution of CDs; (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs; (d) XRD image of CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of CDs and high-resolution peaks of (b) C1s, (c) O1s and (d) N1s.
(a) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CDs; (b) The UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs, ammonium citrate and glutamate.

(a) When the added Cd2+ concentration is 0–25 μM, the fluorescence spectrum of the mixed system excited at a wavelength of 320 nm; (b) The linear fitting relationship between the fluorescence enhancement degree F/F0-1 and the Cd2+ concentration (1–25 μM).

(a) The enhancement of fluorescence after the addition of different metal ions at the same concentration into CDs; (b) The effect of Cd2+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of different metal ions.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) zeta potential of CDs solution before and after adding Cd2+.
The preparation process of CDs and the schematic diagram for the detection of Cd2+ as a turn-on probe.
Determination of Cd2+ in real samples using the turn-on fluorescent probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots as a turn-on fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of Cd2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial optoelectronic engineering and Technology, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial optoelectronic engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial optoelectronic engineering and Technology, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized for the first time using ammonium citrate and glutamic acid as precursors via a one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized carbon dots emit blue fluorescence at 436 nm (excited at 320 nm) and demonstrate excellent photobleaching resistance and fluorescence stability in high salt environments. Within the range of 1–25 μM, the fluorescence of CDs gradually increases with the increasing concentration of Cd2+, reaching a limit of detection as low as 13 nM. This phenomenon could be ascribed to the chelation-enhanced fluorescence, a result of Cd2+ forming complexes with the abundant surface functional groups such as CN–, –COOH, –OH, –NH2 in CDs. Furthermore, this turn-on fluorescent probe has been successfully used for the detection of Cd2+ in tap water and lake water, providing an efficient and sensitive method for the analysis of environmental metals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals have a wide range of applications in industry and agriculture. However, the serious threat of heavy metal ion pollution to human health and ecosystems also follows. Among various heavy metal ions, cadmium is one of the most toxic and harmful heavy metals, widely used in nickel–cadmium batteries, plastic coloring, and cigarettes, etc. [1–4]. Unlike many other heavy metals, cadmium does not participate in biological processes and is not an essential element in the human body. Therefore, even at very low concentrations, cadmium can have adverse effects on organisms through biological accumulation [5,6]. Post-birth, the human body absorbs cadmium from its external surroundings, mainly through food, water, and air. Cadmium can cause irritation to the respiratory tract, and long-term exposure can lead to symptoms such as loss of smell, yellow spots on the gums, or forming yellow circles. Cadmium compounds are not easily absorbed by the gastrointestinal tract, and their accumulation in the liver or kidneys can cause harm, especially with the most obvious damage to the kidneys [7–11]. Japan has experienced “Itai-Itai disease” due to cadmium poisoning [12]. Therefore, establishing economical, convenient, and sensitive methods for detecting Cd2+ is of great importance for environmental protection and human health.

In recent years, the extensive application of carbon quantum dots across diverse disciplines is attributed to their favorable biocompatibility, stable fluorescence, minimal toxicity, and straightforward availability of raw materials [13–15]. Based on the existing technology, researchers have attempted to use carbon dot fluorescent probes for the specific detection of Cd2+, but there are few successfully established strategies. Most of the methods involved prepare carbon dots as “turn-off” fluorescent probes, that is, using the phenomenon of carbon dot fluorescence quenching for analysis. For example, Dai et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots using black fungus powder and ethylenediamine, which could be used for the fluorescence quenching detection of mercury and cadmium ions to achieve the detection purpose [16]. Keerthana et al. synthesized biomass pyrene formaldehyde-carbon quantum dots and developed a ratiometric sensor for the detection of Cd2+ ions in various wastewater [17]. In fact, due to the quenching of carbon dots fluorescence as a result of most heavy metal ions interacting with carbon dots, this type of probe has poor anti-interference properties, which will affect the accuracy of detection. Therefore, it is necessary to develop other types of carbon dot fluorescence probes to supplement the methods for detecting Cd2+ in environmental analysis.

In this work, we used the hydrothermal method to synthesize carbon dots as a fluorescent probe with ammonium citrate and glutamic acid as raw materials. Carbon dots show excellent stability in high-salt environments and long-term UV lamp irradiation. Furthermore, we found that carbon dots can respond to Cd2+ by enhancing fluorescence. Mechanistic studies have shown that based on the principle of chelation-enhanced fluorescence, Cd2+ can coordinate with the abundant CN– and surface functional groups inside CDs to form complexes, enhancing the fluorescence of CDs (Scheme 1 ). The turn-on fluorescent probe can sensitively detect Cd2+ in the range of 1–25 μM, with a detection limit of 13 nM, and is suitable for the determination of Cd2+ in practical environmental water samples.

Ammonium citrate, glutamic acid, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CaCl2, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, AgNO3, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, KCl, CuCl2·2H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, NaCl, disodium hydrogen phosphate, disodium phosphate sodium hydroxide, quinine sulfate, methanol, ethanol, N, N, 2-methylformamide, cyclohexane and acetone were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytical grade reagents and solvents were used in the experiments, and the experimental water was ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ-cm).

A FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) was employed to gauge the fluorescence spectra of the specimens. The UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was acquired using the UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer, from Shimadzu, Japan. High-resolution transmission electron microscope images (HRTEM) of the CDs were taken using the JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using the Frontier FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained from the Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS). The elemental composition of the CDs was analyzed using the ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to record X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were measured with the FLS920 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The Zeta potential was obtained from the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 nanosize and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).

The CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. 3.3 mmol of ammonium citrate and 5.4 mmol of glutamic acid were dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was vigorously shaken at room temperature for 5 min and then sonicated for 10 min to ensure homogeneity. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated from room temperature to 180 °C in an oven and maintained at this temperature for 8 h. After heating, the reaction product was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the upper layer solution was centrifuged at 11,000 r/min under 4 °C for 15 min. The upper clear liquid after centrifugation was then filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, followed by further purification using a 1000 mw dialysis membrane for 24 h to remove small molecular impurities. The prepared carbon dot solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of aqueous solutions of CDs was measured using a reference method. Quinine sulfate standard solution was obtained by dissolving quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid. Quinine sulfate is known to have a fluorescence quantum yield of 55 % when excited at 365 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated by the following formula [18]:
Φ x
=
A s
I s
·
I x
A x
·
n
x
2
n
s
2
·
Φ s
Among them, Φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I is the integrated intensity (integrated area of the fluorescence spectrum under 365-nm excitation), n is the refractive index, and the subscripts of x and s represent the CDs solution and quinine sulfate solution, respectively. In order to make the results accurate, we prepared a series of CDs solution and control solution with different concentrations (concentrations adjusted so that the absorbance was in the range of 0–0.2) [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of detection of Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the selectivity of the prepared CDs for Cd2+, the influence of 11 common interfering metal cations (Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mg2+, Pb2+, K+, Cu2+, Cr3+) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was studied. At room temperature, the blank sample and 10 μM of the 12 metal ions were separately incubated with CDs at the same concentration. The fluorescence spectra of each mixed solution were measured under 320 nm excitation, and the fluorescence intensity at 436 nm was recorded.

A mixture of the 25 times diluted CDs solution, varying concentrations of Cd2+ solution, and pH 7 buffer solution was combined in a ratio of 1:1:3. After a 5-minute reaction, the above samples were subjected to fluorescence spectrum detection, with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, an emission wavelength scanning range of 330–650 nm, a scanning interval of 1 nm and a slit width set to 2/2nm (excitation/emission slit). Each experiment was replicated three times. For determining the limit of detection, the formula is LOD = 3σ/s, with σ symbolizing the standard deviation for three blank samples, and s indicating the standard curve's slope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicality of the method, the sensing strategy was applied to detect Cd2+ in tap water and lake water samples. Samples of tap water were collected from our lab and underwent three filtration processes with a 0.22 μm membrane. Samples of lake water were gathered from the university's lake, centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min, and then filtered three times using a 0.22 μm membrane. In order to obtain the recovery rate of this detection method in actual samples, different concentrations of Cd2+ standard solution were added to environmental water samples, and the analysis was carried out according to section 2.5. Each experiment was replicated three times.

Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to measure the particle morphology and size distribution of CDs. HRTEM image at a scale of 20 nm in Fig. 1 (a) shows the near-spherical shape and effective dispersion of the synthesized carbon dots, with a size range of 2.54–4.57 nm and an average diameter of 3.51 nm (Fig. 1b). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to characterize the surface functional groups of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the absorption bands at 3424 cm−1 and 3146 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H in CDs. The observed peak at 1703 cm−1 s indicative of the uneven stretching vibration of C = O within carboxyl groups. The detected absorption peak at 1588 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C = C, while the peak at 1402 cm−1 is linked to C-N. The stretching vibration of C-O lead to an absorption peak at a lower wavenumber of 1197 cm−1. The findings reveal numerous functional groups, including –NH2, –COOH and –OH, on CDs' surfaces, rendering them both soluble in water and reactive. The depiction of CDs' phase and crystal configuration in Fig. 1 (d) within the 2θ = 0-90° spectrum was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). A strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 29.32° corresponds to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon, suggesting that the product exhibits a standard amorphous carbon configuration along with a low degree of crystallinity [20,21]. In addition, the diffraction peak at 2θ = 40.39° belongs to the C(100) plane [22].

According to the XPS measurements, further analysis of the spectrum was conducted to analyze the surface composition and chemical structure of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the XPS spectrum of CDs exhibits three distinct binding energy peaks at 284.8 eV, 531.93 eV, and 400.11 eV, corresponding to C1s, O1s, and N1s, indicating that the CDs consist of three elements: C, O and N, making up 64.16 %, 29.03 %, and 6.82 % of the total. Each component of the CDs was analyzed using high-resolution energy spectra. Spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) is segmented into three distinct peaks, with the 284.78 eV peak aligns with sp2C (C–C/C = C), and the 285.88 eV and 288.78 eV peaks correspond to C-N/C-O and C = O, respectively. The detailed energy spectrum of O1s as depicted in Fig. 2(c) was re-fitted into three distinct peaks: 531.08 eV for C = O, 531.88 eV for C-O, and 533.08 eV for O–H. Similarly, the energy spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2d) was fitted into 400.08 eV and 401.58 eV peaks, assigned to C-N and N–H, respectively. It can be considered that the FTIR spectra’s characterization findings are corroborated by the results of XPS spectra.

The optimal excitation wavelength, corresponding peak wavelength and synthesis status of carbon dots were determined by measuring the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CDs, UV–Visible absorption spectra of CDs and raw materials. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), CDs’ three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum suggests that there is only one emission center. At an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, CDs display the strongest fluorescence emission, with an emission peak at 436 nm, as depicted in Fig. S1 fluorescence spectrum. In Fig. 3(b), both ammonium citrate (red line) and glutamate (blue line) show no obvious absorption peaks in the wavelength range of 200–500 nm. However, CDs synthesized from the two show a broad absorption band in the range of 290–360 nm, with a peak at 326 nm, attributed to the n-π* transition of sp2 clusters [23]. The change in UV–Visible absorption spectra indicates the successful synthesis of the new substance CDs. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was determined to be 6.5 % with quinine sulfate solution adopted as the standard; the experimental data and results are shown in Fig. S2.

In addition, the effects of (a) NaCl concentration, (b) ambient temperature, (c) UV light exposure, (d) pH of solution, (e) H2O2 concentration and (f) different solvents on the CDs’ fluorescence intensity were also investigated, as shown in Fig. S3. In solutions with different NaCl concentrations (0–1.0 M), only slight fluctuations in the fluorescence intensity of CDs were observed (Fig. S3a). When the ambient temperature was gradually increased from 20 °C to 50 °C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased (Fig. S3b). This is because the increase in temperature causes an increase in non-radiative transition rates, leading to a decrease in fluorescence induced by radiative transitions [24]. In order to achieve better detection performance, the ambient temperature was set at 20 °C, where the CDs exhibited superior optical performance. When continuous exposure of CDs to a UV lamp with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained relatively stable within 120 min (Fig. S3c). When the solution was at neutral pH conditions (pH = 7–8), the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained at a higher level (Fig. S3d). While in acidic or alkaline environments, the surface structure of CDs might be damaged due to the presence of abundant functional groups, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity [25,26]. To maintain a stable pH in the detection system and to ensure that the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains at a higher level, a pH 7 buffer solution is more suitable. In addition, when CDs were co-incubated with high concentrations of H2O2, the images show a slight enhancement of the fluorescence intensity. 100 mM of H2O2 caused less than 25 % enhancement of the CDs fluorescence (Fig. S3e). Moreover, the good stability of the fluorescence of CDs in various soluble organic solvents (methanol (MT), ethanol (EA), N, N, 2-methylformamide (DMF), cyclohexane (CYH) and acetone (CP)) (Fig. S3f) contributed to the formation of their stable surface electronic states [27,28]. These results indicate the feasibility of applying CDs with good fluorescence stability in practical environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd2+ using by the turn-on fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve a better analytical performance, (a) the concentration of CDs used and (b) the incubation time were optimized. As shown in Fig. S4, combined with the fluorescence stability performance of CDs, the optimal detection effect can be obtained when CDs are diluted 25 times, the ambient temperature is 20 °C, the sample pH is 7, and incubated for 5 min before detection. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence enhancement degree of the probe has a good linear relationship with Cd2+ concentration in the range of 1–25 μM (Fig. 4 ). The formula derived from applying this linear correlation is F/F0-1 = 0.14323[C]-0.01433, with R2 of 0.998. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities when Cd2+ is absent and present in the mixed solution, respectively, and [C] is the concentration of Cd2+. After calculation, LOD can reach 13 nM. As shown in Table S1, comparing the result with previously reported methods, our method can achieve a more sensitive effect within a wider detection range. The probe is not only more sensitive than other carbon dot probes for detecting cadmium ion, but is also comparable in level with some other sophisticated measurement methods. Moreover, our result is also below the World Health Organization's safety limit of 0.003 mg/L for cadmium ion content in drinking water.

The fluorescence enhancement of CDs by adding Cd2+ and 11 other common interfering metal cations into CDs were compared, and the influence of Cd2+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of various interfering ions was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), when CDs were respectively incubated with multiple metal ions at the same concentration, it can be seen that only Cd2+ can significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs, while Mg2+ can reduce the fluorescence intensity but its effect is easily interfered. The Fig. 5(b) indicates that in the presence of other metal ions, the fluorescence intensity still undergoes a significant enhancement after the addition of Cd2+ into the CDs solution. Consistent with the selective experiment, although Mg2+ quenches the fluorescence intensity of CDs to some extent, its effect is far less than that of Cd2+ on the fluorescence enhancement of CDs. The competitive experiment demonstrates that under strong interference, the fluorescence probe can still exhibit high selectivity for the detection of Cd2+. The above indicates the significant selectivity and anti-interference ability of the turn-on fluorescence probe for Cd2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible detection mechanism for CDs towards Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, the prevailing view is that the mechanism of metal-enhanced fluorescence is typically categorized into surface plasmon-enhanced fluorescence (SPEF) and chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF). [29]. SPEF refers to the coupling effect between the fluorescent group in the excited state of carbon dots and the metal surface plasmon which increases radiative rate, leading to fluorescence enhancement. This will be manifested as a shift in the absorption peak position, a change in the absorption value, and a significant decrease in the fluorescence lifetime in the spectrum [30–32]. CHEF refers to the formation of a complex between the functional groups on the surface of carbon dots and metal ions, and the chelation between the two will reduce the intramolecular charge transfer, improve the photo-induced electron transfer, and enhance the emission [29,33,34].

Absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes of CDs before and after the addition of Cd2+ were measured, as shown in Fig. S5. It was found that there were no significant changes in either the position or the absorbance values of the absorption peaks. And the fluorescence lifetime only showed a slight increase of 0.13 ns, with no significant decrease, which can be neglected. Therefore, the possibility of the SPEF mechanism can be basically ruled out, and the mechanism of detection is very likely to be CHEF. According to the acid-base theory, Cd2+ belongs to “soft” acid and easily combines with the “soft” base on the surface of carbon dots [35,36]. Therefore, we speculate that the nitrogen atoms abundant in CN– in CDs will coordinate with Cd2+, and –COOH, –OH and –NH2 groups will interact with Cd2+ ions to provide lone pair electrons for the empty orbitals of Cd2+, forming stable Cd/CDs complexes and enhancing fluorescence [37]. In order to verify the formation of Cd/CDs complexes, FTIR and Zeta potential of CDs before and after the addition of Cd2+ were measured. As shown in Fig. 6 a, it can be clearly seen that the intensities of various functional group bonds in the FTIR spectrum of CDs decreased after the addition of Cd2+. After the coordination with metal ions, the metal attracts the chemical bond electrons, weakening the chemical bond strength. Specifically, the two absorption bands near 3400 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H weakened and merged, the peak intensity of C-N at 1402 cm−1 significantly decreased, and the absorption peak of carboxyl group C = O at 1703 cm−1 nearly disappeared. All of these changes indicate the formation of Cd/CDs complexes [5,33]. Additionally, the Zeta potential of CDs increased from −8.15 eV to 0.37 eV (Fig. 6b) after the addition of Cd2+, which is attributed to electrostatic neutralization [38]. These results confirm the chelating interaction between CDs and Cd2+. Therefore, chelation-enhanced fluorescence is likely to be the main mechanism of fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of Cd2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The turn-on fluorescent probe was applied to the detection of Cd2+ in actual samples to analyze the practicality of the method. Laboratory tap water and on-campus lake water, both free from Cd2+ in the original solution, were selected as actual samples. The samples were processed according to the method in “Section 2.6 Detection of Cd2+ in real samples” before use. Within the linear range, the recovery rate of the probe for detecting Cd2+ ranged from 96.5 % to 103.6 % (Table 1 ), and the RSD was within an acceptable range. These results indicate the feasibility of the turn-on fluorescent probe in the practical application for detecting Cd2+.

To sum up, we synthesized carbon dots as an activatable turn-on fluorescence probe by using ammonium citrate and glutamic acid as precursors through a one-step hydrothermal method, for the efficient and sensitive detection of Cd2+. Based on the principle of chelation-enhanced fluorescence and electrostatic neutralization, the fluorescence enhancement of CDs is attributed to the coordination complex formed by the “soft” acid Cd2+ and the “soft” basic and surface functional groups in CDs. During the “turn-on” fluorescence process, the fluorescence probe exhibits a good linear relationship with Cd2+ in the range of 1–25 μM, with LOD as low as 13 nM. Furthermore, the probe has been successfully applied to the detection of Cd2+ in actual samples, yielding satisfactory recovery rates. Overall, this strategy establishes a new method for detecting Cd2+ and holds promising prospects for environmental water quality monitoring.
This work was supported by the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province [KYCX22_2422] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [62375112].

Yan Zhou: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guoqing Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chaoqun Ma: Resources. Taiqun Yang: Validation. Lei Li: Supervision. Jiao Gu: Resources. Chun Zhu: Investigation. Anqi Hu: Visualization. Xin Li: Visualization. Weinan Guan: Visualization. Wei Zhang: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124453.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel N, F co-doped carbon dots to detect sulfide and cadmium ions with high selectivity and sensitivity based on a “turn-off-on” mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zeng, Z. Xu, A. Liu, et al., Novel N, F co-doped carbon dots to detect sulfide and cadmium ions with high selectivity and sensitivity based on a “turn-off-on” mechanism, Dyes Pigm. 203 (2022) 110379.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Replacement of quinolines with isoquinolines affords target metal ion switching from Zn2+ to Cd2+ in the fluorescent sensor TQLN (N, N, N’, N’-tetrakis(2-quinolylmethyl)-2,6-bis(aminomethyl) pyridine)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Mikata, A. Takekoshi, M. Kaneda, et al., Replacement of quinolines with isoquinolines affords target metal ion switching from Zn2+ to Cd2+ in the fluorescent sensor TQLN (N, N, N’, N’-tetrakis(2-quinolylmethyl)-2,6-bis(aminomethyl) pyridine), Dalton Trans. 46 (2017) 632-637.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in cadmium fluorescent and colorimetric probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Shi, Z. Huang, A. Wu, et al., Recent progress in cadmium fluorescent and colorimetric probes, RSC Adv. 11 (2021) 29632-29660.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Europium doped silicon quantum dot as a novel FRET based dual detection probe: sensitive detection of tetracycline, zinc, and cadmium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhou, R. Zhao, S. Liu, et al., Europium doped silicon quantum dot as a novel FRET based dual detection probe: sensitive detection of tetracycline, zinc, and cadmium, Small Methods 5 (2021) 2100812.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots codoped with nitrogen and phosphorus as a turn-on fluorescent probe for cadmium(II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Lin, Y. Wang, Y. Xiao, et al., Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots codoped with nitrogen and phosphorus as a turn-on fluorescent probe for cadmium(II), Microchim. Acta 186 (2019) 147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A selective fluorescent sensor for imaging Cd2+ in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Peng, J. Du, J. Fan, et al., A selective fluorescent sensor for imaging Cd2+ in living cells, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 129 (2007) 1500-1501.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward selective, sensitive, and discriminative detection of Hg2+ and Cd2+ via pH-modulated surface chemistry of glutathionecapped gold nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Huang, S. Li, N. Gao, et al., Toward selective, sensitive, and discriminative detection of Hg2+ and Cd2+ via pH-modulated surface chemistry of glutathionecapped gold nanoclusters, Analyst. 140 (2015) 7313-7321.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Arsenic and heavy metal (cadmium, lead, mercury and nickel) contamination in plant-based foods, Plant and Human

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hussain, Z. Rengel, M. Qaswar, et al., Arsenic and heavy metal (cadmium, lead, mercury and nickel) contamination in plant-based foods, Plant and Human Health, 2 (2019) 447-490.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensors for the environmental pollutant detection: Are we already there?
